speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Bernice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. My name is Bernice Krantz. It's December the 1st, 1995. I'm conducting an interview with Leon Greenman in London, England. And this is tape number five. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's April--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It's January, '45. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We're still marching out to work. And I find myself digging in a-- in a big gap in the ground-- about a quarter of the size of this room here. And I always thought, why-- why must I dig so deep? Why did-- they didn't even have-- well, I said, maybe the tanks of the Russians when they come in. They might fall into it-- something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm digging. And then I'm thinking again. And then all of a sudden I hear a man say-- a man's voice say, [GERMAN]. That means rest.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I looks up, and I sees an old man in a SS uniform-- too big for him-- and a gun over his shoulder. I look up. The SS tells me to-- to rest. So he says-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --take it easy-- like, you know. So I took it easy. He says, [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: First he says, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. He lost two sons on the Russian front. And I'm from-- I said, good for you. [LAUGHTER] Then he says, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I've got no tobacco. And he's got a pipe in his hand-- in his mouth.

speaker: interviewee
text: So for a-- for a second, I got a pity with the man. Now, I used to find-- now and then-- pieces of cigarette in the ground. And I used to put-- I don't-- didn't smoke myself. I put them in my back pocket. And I said-- I thought to meself I might-- might give the little bit of tobacco I got. So I gave him a little bit of tobacco-- you know-- reached up to him-- and smoked.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I thought to myself, you silly ass. He's-- he's-- after all, he's your manager. He's your enemy. So-- anyhow, l forgot about it. That was him. They had no young soldiers more left. They were all fighting-- old men-- old-- camp guard. Anyhow, the order came then-- no more work. Stay in your barracks. Get ready for a journey. You're allowed to take one blanket along.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when was this? Still January?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is January '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians were nearing Auschwitz. And yes, we're laying there-- and on the bed I laid. I thought to myself, well-- --God knows where we're going. But the Germans are losing the war, because I had seen the train tracks coming back from the front with burned-out tanks and parts of airplanes. And then the next day, I saw freshly armaments go into the front, fighting the Russians. If they came in burnt, they did good. When they went there with fresh weapons, I warned myself. Oh, it's not over yet. Anyhow, I found a long coat with-- with sleeves. And I took the-- the-- lining out and put them around my feet, and then my feet into those shoes-- boots-- bad boots, they were. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah. Something jumps into my mind right in the beginning. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One of the Dutchmen does something wrong-- I don't know what. But they beat him up in such a way that he can't forget it. And he's the first one to die. His name was [NON-ENGLISH]. He was a hairdresser in the Hague. He had family in Rotterdam. And the family knows me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- was this-- he got beaten in January of '45?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, earlier.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- this was in the beginning of Birkenau. But that picture comes into my mind now-- the first Dutchman to die over 50. The second one said, good night, boys. See you in the morning. And he was laying in front of us on a wooden-- just wooden beds-- no mattress-- wooden beds and a blanket just over you. And the next morning, we woke up. He didn't. He was dead. He died during the night-- probably a cold or a heart attack. And after liberation, I found a brother of him in Rotterdam. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This man who died was living opposite me in the same street in Rotterdam where I used to live. And I knew the family. And after the war, walking and finding a street where this-- the brother of him-- of the dead man-- had a tailor shop. I went in there, and I had to talk to him. And then he said to me, my brother also was taken away. And it always hinders me. He said, I can't think where he is. But he's gone-- what he-- where he is.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, you really want to know what happened to your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, yeah-- I would very much like.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, you're sure?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, yes, of course. I can't stop thinking-- if I know something, then I'm-- I know what to think. I said, well, I'll tell you then. Your brother died-- so and so. And so he said, oh. Now I'm satisfied. And I told him. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we're marching-- we're not marching out to work. But then the second day they said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Get out-- out of the barracks-- 10,000 men. And we're standing outside the barracks. And we had to march back to Auschwitz. How far was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: About three miles-- three-- three, four kilometers-- and it's freezing cold-- January '45. And I thought to myself, oh-- that's it. It's the beginning of the end. Soon it'll be over. And we marched to Auschwitz. And in Auschwitz, I see thousands of men from all the barracks around there-- little ones, big ones. They all were assembling. It looked like in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I see a friend of mine from Rotterdam. He was a boxer. And I said, we see by now they're in Rotterdam, yes? Yes? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] from Auschwitz begins the death march to Gleiwitz-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] --freezing weather, snow falling, and high snow on the ground. And if you can't walk, or you can't keep up, they shot you. I've seen it done. They had no mercy with us-- no pity whatsoever. So you had to keep on walking, walking, walking, walking, because the SS was escaping from the Russians. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] You try to run away-- I saw it. The SS put his gun on the shoulder, and the man running-- where the beautiful white snow-- shot-- finished-- didn't come home. And we're walking, walking. And I gets a pain in my legs. And I can't-- I can't-- hardly walk anymore. And I walk in the middle-- five-- two on one-- each side. And one-- the man, the young man on my side here-- he must be a young man-- about 30-- got hold of my arm, and I nearly tumbled. And he pulled me up. Come on! Come on, Leon! Come on. Come on, Englander-- Englishman-- come on! [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I tumble along. And then I nearly fell again. And he pulled me up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And whenever I saw the guard coming towards me, I did as if nothing was the matter. And when he went by and came back again-- I saw his back-- he couldn't see me. I tried to make it a bit easy. And I'm here. If I would have dropped, I wouldn't have been here. I would have been shot and finished with. I got my life to thank on that Frenchman. And probably, the Frenchman never got home. But, I don't know who he is. He must know me. But I don't know who he is. I don't know his name. I put an advert in a French paper. But I don't know who it is. If he's still alive, and he remembers-- if he remembers that-- I would like to get in touch. Now, we're marching. We arrive at Gleiwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] How long were you marching for?

speaker: interviewee
text: 90 kilometers.

speaker: interviewee
text: 90 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: All day. We had a small rest in an old factory where they made bricks-- a brick-- a baking factory. We were-- well, I-- I was so weak. We didn't care for eating no more or drink. There was nothing to eat-- nothing to drink. We only want-- I wanted to sleep. I wanted to rest. And then a little later, we woke up one hour. Then we had to get out and continue our walk. And we arrived at Gleiwitz. Gleiwitz is only a small camp-- still in Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was a fighting to get into the barracks out of the cold-- fighting them-- hitting one another to get in. And I didn't want to be hit, so I-- I walked back-- backwards. I didn't want to go into the lot of people. And I found a heap of wood or something. And I sat down on it. And I pulled my blanket around me. And I wanted to sleep. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as I dozed off, I saw this picture of this Dutchman saying good night and freezing to death. And I thought to myself, I don't want to freeze. I want to see my wife and child. I can't do this. I've got to-- I got-- so I woke up out of my semi-sleep and walked around the barracks until I found a barrack with a window open. And I looked inside. And there were a lot of men laying all over the place on top of one another-- sleeping. I crawled through the window and dropped onto the men. And I slept. We only stayed about 3, or 4, or 5 days in Gleiwitz. And then we had to assemble-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] --on the platform. But before that, I want to tell you-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the order came. Those that can't walk can stay. Oh, I thought to myself, that's good. I don't have to walk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went-- but you had to present yourself to a doctor. So I went into-- to a barrack where a doctor was. I thought it was Dr. [? Zilberman-- ?] that kind of name-- [? Zilberman, ?] I think. And he says, if you stay here, it's your own-- he had seen me before in the camps. I had been in the hospital maybe 3, or 4, or 5, times. He said, it's your own responsibility if you stay here. It won't be any good.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, well, I can't walk. And I'll try to stay here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were sent to a barrack. And in that barrack, I went. And there were a lot of men already in there-- and 4 or 5 coming in-- 2, 1-- all that wanted to stay behind-- in one barrack.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was also the Dutchman there, [? Judah ?] [? Solomon ?]. He was a street photographer in Rotterdam. He used to live not far from me in the same turning. And he had a bandage around his head. And he stood in front of a fire-- one of those fire ovens. And he had his bread-- a piece of bread-- on top of there. He was toasting it. And I had a talk with him. I said, well, we're nearly-- we're nearly home, and all this, and all that. And then, I walked away. And I heard a lot of noise. And I looked around. They had stolen his piece of bread, and they were trying to fight him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] All of a sudden, a man jumped on me and got his hands around my throat. And I couldn't make him understand. And I didn't understand what he was doing. So another-- a couple of other fellas got him off of me. And it appeared that he said, he has stolen my piece of margarine. That wasn't so. And I thought he was after a silver spoon I'd found in the barrack-- beautiful spoon-- all hand-engraved. I had it in my pocket. I'd found it in one of the barracks. I thought he was after that. So that was a misunderstanding.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the barrack-- so in the afternoon, the window opens. And an SS in front of the window with a leg of a chair-- and he knocks it on this window sill. He says, [GERMAN]. So he says, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. All German-- non-Jews outside. So some of them go out. Yeah-- all-- [SPEAKING GERMAN], French-- non-Jews are out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he goes right through them. And then I says, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. He says, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Ah, you're a Jew. And he closed the window. And there I am.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I start thinking, all the Jews in here-- what are they going to do with us-- shoot us or kill us? I don't want to have that. So I tried to go through one door out of the barrack, and there's a guard there with a gun. So I walked back. Then I tried the other door.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm outside, but the gates were-- were locked-- wooden-- wooden kind of gates with a chain around it. And outside there were a guard. And through-- this in the gate-- I could see a lot of my pals from the barracks where I-- and a lot of people I knew. So I start shouting. And some of them, they didn't take a notice. And one man was there-- Carl. Carl, his name was. Carl-- poor buddy-- he was a kind man. And I used to shave him in the barrack.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I shouted, Carl! Carl! Until he looked around and heard me-- and he came. I made gestures. He says to the guard, what's this man doing in there? He's a good worker. So the guard says, aw, he can't walk. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. So I said-- I said, ya-- yes, I can walk. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And I jumped up and walked around a bit in the space. And my luck, he took the chain off the gate. And I was outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I was outside, a few yards away from me I saw another friend of mine from Rotterdam-- shook hands. He looked strong and all right. And we knew him. I used to work with him in Rotterdam. And he used to be my pal.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, we're going to see one another in Rotterdam-- don't forget. Yes, yes. I never saw him again. I guess a lot of them-- or some of them-- were shot, trying to escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, back to the platform on Gleiwitz. That evening-- getting dark-- along came a whole row of open cattle trucks. And within minutes, we had to be in there. Now, the strong ones were quickly in there. But the weak ones-- and the legs-- like myself-- I couldn't. We couldn't climb. But the guns of the SS-- they're on your head, in your guts, I mean-- in your back, and your side, and your ribs-- until the platform was cleared. And everything had to be quick, quick, quick, quick. And there we were. And in the wagon where I was sitting were far too many people. Now, the fit men took charge of us. And they counted over 140 men. There was not enough room. You couldn't sit next to one another. You couldn't stand next to one another. So you dropped on top of one another And I was sitting on my-- on my bum like that. And left and right I had two Dutchmen-- two men from Amsterdam. Now, one, I don't remember. But the other one was my pal in Monowitz. He was my size-- much stronger than me. And he used to say to me, the Germans are not going to kill me. I'll be home. I'll get home. And he never got home. His name was Barron Dinstof. He's still got a brother in Amsterdam. But I didn't search for this brother. I didn't want to tell him what happened to his brother. In case he does hear me, then he knows what happened to Barron Dinstof-- who owes me still a quarter bread. I gave him 20 canteen cigarettes-- bad stuff. He said, give them to me, and I'll get some bread. And we'll share. It never came as far as that. Well, that was Monowitz. From Monowitz to Gleiwitz-- and from Gleiwitz we got in the trucks. And now starts a five day journey. Those trucks had too many men. Every morning when it stopped, four or five men came out of the trucks every morning. They were-- they were dead-- choked or frozen to death. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was my turn one morning to-- the trains went during the night. But during the day, they didn't pass through the towns-- could the population-- should not see what happened in those carriages. So it stopped. And one of those mornings, it was my turn to go out of-- get out of the truck with a little [NON-ENGLISH]-- is an aluminum container-- to get some snow from between the rails-- because there was nothing to eat, nothing to drink. So it was my turn to get out. And I was bending down and shoveling with my spoon-- snow into it. And then I saw a couple of Wellingtons next to me. And I looked up. It was an SS guard.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he dragged me up. He said, what are you doing, you pig? [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Shit, he said. Get up. What are you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, well, give us something to eat. There's nothing else to eat but-- but-- but snow.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, what eat? He says, carry your comrades! So I looked-- a whole row of men carrying bodies-- one man on each arm, and one man on each leg-- carrying the dead men out of the trucks. And I thought to myself, I'm not going to do that. So I bent down, got all of my-- my-- the snow-- climbed up against my truck, handed over the-- the snow to one of the men in there. I said, here. And I wanted to get in. And that guard had saw me. And he pulled me down.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, carry your comrades. And I carried several of them-- maybe six or eight bodies-- to the last-- the last carriage was a big round roofed one. And inside-- of what I could see-- was the men beautifully placed on top of one another-- on and over, like loaves of bread. Well, I've seen a lot, but this-- I couldn't stand the thought of that. And I walked around the other side of the train and found my own truck. And I-- I crawled in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to the boys, what I've seen now, it's terrible, terrible, terrible. But nobody took notice, because we were really-- what were we? Half dead? And I said, where's my snow? He said, what snow? My friend said, what snow? There's no snow.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, but, I gave you a bucket of snow.

speaker: interviewee
text: You sure? Well, it's gone. So I took my spoon and tried to scrape some snow off a blanket of another prisoner. And he's going to fight me, because I was stealing his snow. That's how we were at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was one man amongst-- and also out-- the same street where I lived-- a friend of my father. I woke up one night, and I had my foot underneath his arm. I couldn't feel the leg anymore. And I said, Yop-- his name was Yop Tabum. And his son was [? Yopi ?] Tabum. I said, Yop, let go of my leg. I can't feel my leg. Oh, no. He says, stay where you are. And I was laying upside down like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, let my leg go. You get off of my leg. So my friend, Barron Dinstof, he says, let Leon's leg go, or I'll give you one. So he let go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I took my boots off, and I couldn't get them on again. My foot swelled up all of a sudden. I had elephant feet-- rotten elephant feet-- rotten feet from the death march. And he got up, this fellow. He said, you people make a lot of noise, a lot of trouble. I'll tell you what I'll do. I'm going to jump out of the train. I'll be home before you people get home. And he did. He walked over us-- over the men-- jumped out of the train, and I never saw him again. And I never saw his son neither. They're all gone. All my pals were gone. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, this journey took five days. The fifth evening, in between that journey, one night they called out-- one dark night they called out, how many men? And somebody said 125 were left in our-- and they threw loaves of bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a fighting to get a piece of bread. And I couldn't. I was weak. I was-- I was half finished. Because I kept on telling my friend, Barron, when you get to Holland, go to Rotterdam. Go to that-- that address. I kept on repeating it to him. And tell my father that I lived. I said, if I die today, then tell him that I lived at that and that date. Because I promised my father I'd get back, or make a fight of it. And he used to say, yes, I know, Leon. I know. I'll tell you. I promised. Well, he got up, and he came back with two pieces of bread. And he said, one piece for me. I had a piece of bread. And then there was one night in those five days, there was-- he said-- hot soup was being given. And somehow, hot soup was given. But we didn't have the-- the enamel bowls anymore. And whoever had one of those had to share his soup. So-- oh, I was-- I was feverish. And oh, soup meant for me-- something wet. So I got up, and I said to one of the men there with the soup, I said, let's-- let me drink a little. So I took two sips. It was so hot, I couldn't enjoy it. So I dropped back again into my position where I sat. Anyhow, the days went on. And the fifth day, we arrived in Buchenwald. We stopped at a place, and it was Buchenwald. And the fit men jumped out. And the-- the dead and the dying kept laying. And I was one of those that hardly could move.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was helped down-- where I called out to my friends, help me down. Put me up against the wall, so they can see me. He did that. And I didn't see him no more. He didn't come home. That was Barron Dinstof-- translated as Barron Tuesday. So there were hundreds of us that wanted to get into a barrack. I mean, we were pulling up to the barrack door. And the door opened. And out came one in a green coat that were the camp police of Buchenwald-- with a stick in his hand. And then he start hitting. So then-- oh-- we fell back. And I fell on the ground, and they were trampling on me. So I shouted out. And they picked me up, and they all had to-- got back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm by myself. I'm by myself. Dammit. I'm going to get in. And I walked, and the door opened. I got in. And I was inside. I was dirty from top to bottom. I didn't care anymore. I didn't care for the spoon. I didn't care for the-- for nothing. Undressed-- and there I stood. And as I looked around, I saw men-- skin and bones-- crawling over the floor to a leaking tap in the corner. They pulled themselves up and let the drops of water in their mouths. And then they let go. And then other prisoners got hold of the body and put them in the corner-- like eels in a fish market. And then, I had to share a Lysol bath with some men. And then we were taken to a barrack. And three days later, the doctors came in-- prisoners [? that ?] [? were ?] doctors-- examined us. And all according to what was wrong with you-- they sent you to various barracks. I had rotten feet. I had holes in my heels. So I was taken to the barrack for rotten feet. Some other Dutchmen were there as well. And one of the young Dutchmen, [INAUDIBLE], he had his bunk on the slant-- his bed on the slant of mine. These were nice beds, they were. They must have been former SS beds-- barracks. And we talked. And I said to him, well, our people are on our way home now. It's all over. We'll be free soon. And this was a fella. I don't think he was 20 years of age. He was an amateur boxer. And he said, yes, he said, I know. But I won't see my father, mother, and my sister anymore. I said, well, you don't know. I said, I'm going to see my wife and child. Oh, no. He says, no. They're all gone. I said, well, I don't think so. I'm hoping to see [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, well, tell me then. When did you see your-- your wife and child for the last time? Then I'll tell you what I told you-- the truck-- going away.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, shut up.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, what's the matter?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, I worked near the gas chambers for a long time, and I saw many people go in. But I never saw anybody come out. And that's where your wife and child is now. Don't talk about it. And I still believed that my wife and child was alive until the last moment. Because after liberation, I thought probably the Swedish or the Russian Red Cross, the Norwegian, or Danish Red Cross, had taken away the women and the children. That's how I kidded myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you had to live in hope.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hope. And I had pneumonia once. And I fell asleep. And in my-- fever, I had a dream. I saw a lot of family and friends, but I didn't see my wife and child anywhere. And I woke up. And I said to a Frenchman next to me-- the Frenchman could talk English. He was a translator of films in English-- French, and French into English. I said, when the soup comes, you can have my soup.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, aren't you hungry anymore? I said no. I'm-- I'm-- I'll stop eating. He said, well, tell me then. What's the matter?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I had a dream. And I'm now sure my wife and child is no more alive. And I don't want to go on. And I told him my dream.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, suppose your wife and child is alive, and you're not there. That's not fair, is it? And I came to my senses again. And I ate my soup.
